titleStmt.title: An Axis Symmetric 2D Description of the Dynamics of a Single Crystal Micro Fiber Growth from the Melt by Micro-Pulling-Down Method. Part.1

analytic.title: An Axis Symmetric 2D Description of the Dynamics of a Single Crystal Micro Fiber Growth from the Melt by Micro-Pulling-Down Method. Part.1

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

keywords.term: micro fiber growth from the melt

div.p: In this paper the first part of an axis symmetric 2D description of a single crystal micro fiber growth process by micro-pulling-down (╬╝-PD) method is presented. The description concerns the following aspects: the free surface equation and the pressure difference across the free surface (section 2); limits of the pressure difference p across the free surface (section 3); the fiber radius change rate, due to the change of the pressure difference p across the free surface , and the fiber radius size control (section 4);the static stability of the free surface (section 5). The above aspects are illustrated numerically in the case of the growth of a Si micro-fiber of radius 0.0001[m] by using the Maple 17 software. This description can be helpful in the better understanding of the growth process and in the automation of manufacturing.

div.head: INTRODUCTION
div.p: The near one-dimensional single crystal fibers have attracted some attention on the applications of optical and electronic devices [1][2][3]. There are several methods for growing fiber crystals [4]. The micro-pulling down (╬╝-PD) process, a variant of the inverse edge-defined film-fed growth, developed by Fukuda's laboratory in Japan [5][6][7][8][9], has been shown promising in producing single crystal fibers with good diameter control and concentration uniformity. According to [10], for this process some simple theoretical analysis for the operation limits [11] and solute distribution [12,13] have been performed and no detailed modeling has been conducted. In [14] the meniscus shape and size, appearing in ╬╝-PD were evaluated in function of pressure difference, which incorporates the hydrostatic pressure of the melt column situated behind the meniscus and the pressure in front of the free surface of the meniscus. In [15] some results from [14] were used in the analysis of the effect of the pressure difference across the free surface on the shape and size of the meniscus in an NdYAG microfiber growth from the melt by ╬╝-PD method.

div.head: II. THE FREE SURFACE EQUATION
div.p: The free surface of the meniscus (see Fig. 1) is described mathematically by the Young-Laplace equation [16,17]:
div.formula: b a P P R R ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΔ╖ ΎΔ╖ ΎΔ╕ ΎΔ╢ ΎΔπ ΎΔπ ΎΔρ ΎΔο ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ 2 1 1 1 ΎΒπ (1)
div.p: Here: ΎΒπ is the melt surface tension;

denote the main normal curvatures of the free surface at a point M of the free surface; a P is the pressure in front of the free surface; b P is the pressure behind the free surface. (Fig. 1). The pressure b P behind the free surface is the sum of the hydrodynamic pressure m p in the meniscus melt (due to the thermal convection), the Marangoni pressure M p (due to the Marangoni convection) and the hydrostatic pressure of the melt column behind the free surface equal to
div.formula: ) ( m h z g ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΒ▓
div.p: (see Fig. 1). Here: ΎΒ▓ denotes the melt density;

g is the gravity acceleration; z is the coordinate of M with respect to the Oz axis, directed vertically downwards; m h denotes the melt column height between the horizontal crucible melt level and the shaper bottom level (Fig. 1). The pressure difference b a P P ΎΑφ across the free surface, denoted usually by p ΎΒΕ , according to the above considerations is:
div.formula: ) ( m M m a h z g p p P p ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΒΕ ΎΒ▓ . (2
div.p: The part p of p ΎΒΕ is given by:
div.formula: M m m a p p h g P p ΎΑτ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΒ▓(3)
div.p: is independent of the spatial coordinate z (it can depend on the moment of time t ) and the major part of p is
div.formula: m a h g P ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΑφ ΎΒ▓ because the sum M m p p ΎΑτ in general is small with respect to m g h g p ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΑφ ΎΒ▓ .
div.p: For this reason thereafter is assumed that p is given by:
div.formula: m a h g P p ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΒ▓ (4)
div.p: Remark that p given by ( 4) can be controlled by With this approximation the Young-Laplace free surface equation ( 1) can be written as:
div.formula: p z g R R ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΔ╖ ΎΔ╖ ΎΔ╕ ΎΔ╢ ΎΔπ ΎΔπ ΎΔρ ΎΔο ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒπ 2 1 1 1 (5)
div.p: Therefore, for an axis-symmetric meniscus free surface the differential equation of the meridian curve is given by:
div.formula: ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΒδ ΎΒζ ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΒδ ΎΒζ ' ' 1 1 ' 1 " 2 2 3 2 z z r z p z g z ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΒπ ΎΒ▓ d c R r R ΎΓμ ΎΓμ(6)
div.p: where c R is the crystal radius , d R is the shaper radius and h R is the capillary channel radius.
The function ΎΑρ ΎΑσ r z , describing the meridian curve profile, verifies the following boundary conditions:
div.formula: ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΑρ ΎΑσ g c R z ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ 2 / tan ' ; 0 ) ( ΎΑ╛ ΎΑ╜ c c h R z (7) ΎΑρ ΎΑσ c d R z ΎΒκ tan ' ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ; ΎΑρ ΎΑσ 0 ΎΑ╜ d R z (8) ΎΑρ ΎΑσ r z is strictly decreasing on ΎΒδ ΎΒζ d c R R , .(9)
div.p: The first condition in (7) expresses that at the three phase point ) , (
div.formula: c c h R
div.p: , where the thermal conditions for solidification have to be realized ( c h is the crystallization front level), the angle, between the tangent line to the meridian curve of the free surface and the vertical ,is equal to the growth angle g ΎΒκ . If this condition is satisfied during the whole process, then the fiber diameter is constant. The second condition in (7) expresses that the coordinate of the crystallization front level is 0
div.formula: ΎΑ╛ c h .
div.p: The first condition in (8) expresses that at the point
where the meridian curve touches the outer edge of the shaper, the catching angle is equal to c ΎΒκ .
The second condition in (8) expresses that at the point
the meridian curve is fixed to the outer edge of the shaper.
Condition (9) expresses that the meniscus shape is relatively simple.

Beside the conditions ( 6), ( 7), ( 8) and ( 9) the function ΎΑρ ΎΑσ r z describing the meridian curve profile, has to minimize the free energy functional of the melt column behind the free surface given by :
div.formula: ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΒδ ΎΒζ ΎΔ▓ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔ╛ ΎΔ╜ ΎΔ╝ ΎΔχ ΎΔφ ΎΔυ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╜ d c R R dr r z p z g z z I 2 2 1 2 2 1 ' 1 ΎΒ▓ ΎΒπ (10)
div.p: This last condition is called the static stability of the axis symmetric free surface and in real world only statically stable capillary free surfaces exists..

div.head: III. LIMITS OF THE PRESSURE DIFFERENCE p
div.p: If p is arbitrary, then it can happen that there is no function
div.formula: ) (r z defined for ΎΒδ ΎΒζ d c R R r , ΎΔΟ
div.p: which verifies ( 6) -( 9) and supplementary 0 ) ( " ΎΑ╛ r z . This means that there is no convex static axis-symmetric meniscus. The following statement is a necessary condition for the existence of a function ) (r z having the properties ( 6) -(9) and 0
div.formula: ) ( " ΎΑ╛ r z for ΎΒδ ΎΒζ d c R R r , ΎΔΟ . Statement 1 For 2 / ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΑ╝ ΎΑτ g c
div.p: , in order to have an axissymmetric meniscus free surface with convex meridian curve on ΎΒδ ΎΒζ
div.formula: d d c R n R R , / ΎΑ╜
div.p: the pressure across the free surface has to be in the range given by:
div.formula: ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΑρ ΎΑσ c d g d g c g d g d c d g c R n n R p n R n n R g n n R ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΓμ ΎΓμ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ (11) where 1 / ΎΑ╛ ΎΑ╜ c d R R n .
div.p: The proof of this statement can be found in the Appendix. In case of a Si microfiber for the following numerical data: h ,but the range where the value of p has to be is large and we don't know which value of p is appropriate. Moreover, is not sure that there exists a value of p in the above range for which an axis symmetric meniscus with convex meridian curve exists (the condition ( 11) is only necessary).
div.formula: ΎΒδ ΎΒζ ; 10 2 4 m R d ΎΑφ ΎΓ┤ ΎΑ╜ ΎΒδ ΎΒζ;

ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΑρ ΎΑσ g d g d c d g c n R n n R g n n R n L ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ cos 2 / tan 1 cos 1 2 / ) ( 1 ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╜ (12) c d g d g c R n n R n L ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ sin sin 1 2 / ) ( 2 ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╜(13)
div.p: In order to answer these questions the following initial value problem:
div.formula: ΎΔψ ΎΔψ ΎΔψ ΎΔχ ΎΔψ ΎΔψ ΎΔψ ΎΔφ ΎΔυ ΎΑ╜ ΎΑ╜ ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╜ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ c d d R R z r p z g dr d dr dz ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ) ( , 0 ) ( ) 14 ( tan

div.head: IV. EQUATION OF THE FIBER RADIUS CHANGE RATE DUE TO THE CHANGE OF THE PRESSURE DIFFERENCE AND FIBER RADIUS SIZE CONTROL
div.p: Starting from the condition of growth angle constancy, according to [17] , the following equation of the fiber radius change rate, due to the pressure difference perturbation p , is obtained: the solutions of the initial value problems ( 16), ( 17) and ( 18) are represented on Fig. 3 with green, blue and red respectively. On Fig. 3 can be seen that:
div.formula: 0001 . 0 ) 0 ( )) 2 / ( ) , 0001 . 0 ( tan( / ΎΑ╜ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ c g c R p v dt dR ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ(15)
div.p: -if in the first 0.
div.formula: ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ c g c R t p v dt dR ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ (21) For ] / [ 0001 . 0 s m v ΎΑ╜ and ΎΑρ ΎΑσ t t p g 600 sin 2 / )) ( , 0001 . 0 ( ΎΑτ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ0001 . 0 ) 0 ( )) 2 / ( )) ( , 0001 . 0 ( tan( / ΎΑ╜
div.p: the fiber radius evolution during the first second can be seen in Fig. 6a.On Fig. 6.b the plate half thickness evolution during the first second is presented for the already considered instantaneous perturbations and for the above considered oscillatory perturbation.

div.head: V. STATIC STABILITY OF THE FREE SURFACE
div.p: This section deals with the static stability of the free surface [14]. The following statement is a sufficient condition of static stability of the free surface.
div.formula: Statement 2 If 1 / ΎΑ╛ ΎΑ╜ c d R R n satisfies 2 / 1 2 / 1 2 / 3 2 / 1 2 / 1 sin ) 1 ( g R n n d g ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╝ ΎΑφ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒ░ (22)
div.p: then the axis-symmetric meniscus is stable, i.e. ) (r z minimizes the free energy functional
div.formula: ) (z I . If 1 / ΎΑ╛ ΎΑ╜ c d R R n satisfies 2 / 1 2 / 1 2 / 3 2 / 1 2 / 3 cos 2 ) 1 ( g R n n d c ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╛ ΎΑφ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒ░ (23)
div.p: then the axis-symmetric meniscus is unstable, i.e. ) (r z is not a minimum for the free energy functional ) (z I . In the case of the Si fiber considered in section 3 we have:
div.formula: 7071067814 0 / ) 1 ( 2 / 1 . n n ΎΑ╜ ΎΑφ and 198643944 7 sin 2 / 1 2 / 1 2 / 3 2 / 1 . g R d g ΎΑ╜ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒ░
div.p: . Therefore, the static free surface is stable.

div.head: VI. CONCLUSIONS
div.p: In the framework of the presented description, the following quantities, related to the micro fiber growth process from the melt by pulling down method, can be computed:

ΎΑφ pressure difference across the free surface for create a stable static meniscus appropriate for the growth of a fiber having a prior given radius. ΎΑφ pressure difference across the free surface for controlling the fiber radius size during the growth

div.head: ANNEXE
div.p: Proof of the Statement 1.
div.formula: Let 2 / ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΑ╝ ΎΑτ g c , n R R d c / ΎΑ╜ , n ΎΑ╝ 1 and ) (r z defined for ΎΒδ ΎΒζ d d c R n R R r , / ΎΑ╜ ΎΔΟ
div.p: which verifies ( 7), ( 12) -( 14) and
div.formula: 0 ) ( " ΎΑ╛ r z
div.p: The function defined as:
div.formula: )) ( ' arctan( ) ( r z r ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΒκ for ΎΒδ ΎΒζ d d c R n R R r , / ΎΑ╜ ΎΔΟ verifies )) ( tan( 1 )) ( cos( 1 ) ( ) ( ' r r r p r z g r ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╝ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╝ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╜ with 1 / ΎΑ╛ ΎΑ╜ c d R R n .
div.p: So, the right hand side of the inequality ( 12) is proven.
In order to obtain the left hand side of (11) remark first the inequality:
div.formula: ). cos( ) ( cos 2 / )) ' ( sin( ' ) ' ( ) ' ( cos 2 / g c c c c d g c c d g c R R z g R R r r r z g r R R p ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╛ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╜ The term ) ( c R z g ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΒ▓
div.p: can be evaluated applying the mean value theorem for the function
div.formula: ) (r z on ΎΒδ ΎΒζ d c R R , . It follows that there exists ΎΒδ ΎΒζ d c R R r , ' ' ΎΔΟ such that ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ) 2 ( tan ) ( ' ) ( ' ' ' ) ( ) ( ) ( g c d d c d c d c d R R R z R R r z R R R z R z ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╛ ΎΑφ ΎΑ╜ ΎΑφ 0 ) ( ΎΑ╜ d R z .
div.p: So, for p the following relations hold:
div.formula: ) cos( ) 2 tan( 1 cos 1 2 / ) cos( ) tan( ) ( cos 2 / g d g d c d g c g c c c d c c d g c n R n n R g n n R R R R g R R p ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒκ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒκ ΎΒ░ ΎΒκ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╛ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╛
div.p: This means that the left hand side of ( 12) is proven.

div.head: Proof of the Statement 2.
div.p: Since ( 7) is the Euler equation [27] Chapter2 for the free energy functional (15), in this case it is sufficient to investigate the Legendre and Jacobi conditions [27] Chapter 8. Consider for that the function
div.formula: ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΒδ ΎΒζ r z p z g z r z z F ΎΔΩ ΎΔ╛ ΎΔ╜ ΎΔ╝ ΎΔχ ΎΔφ ΎΔυ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑ╜ 2 2 / 1 22

ΎΑρ ΎΑσ ΎΒδ ΎΒζ 0 ' 1 2 / 3 2 ΎΑ╛ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΑφ z r ΎΒπ which is satisfied . The Jacobi equation 0 ' ' 2 2 2 2 2 ΎΑ╜ ΎΔ║ ΎΔ╗ ΎΔ╣ ΎΔς ΎΔτ ΎΔσ ΎΔΩ ΎΓ╢ ΎΓ╢ ΎΑφ ΎΔΩ ΎΔ║ ΎΔ╗ ΎΔ╣ ΎΔς ΎΔτ ΎΔσ ΎΔ╖ ΎΔ╖ ΎΔ╕ ΎΔ╢ ΎΔπ ΎΔπ ΎΔρ ΎΔο ΎΓ╢ ΎΓ╢ ΎΓ╢ ΎΑφ ΎΓ╢ ΎΓ╢ ΎΒρ ΎΒρ z F dr d z z F dr d z F in this case becomes 0 ' ] ) ' ( 1 [ 2 / 3 2 ΎΑ╜ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΔ╖ ΎΔ╖ ΎΔ╕ ΎΔ╢ ΎΔπ ΎΔπ ΎΔρ ΎΔο ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ ΎΒρ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒρ ΎΒπ r g z r dr d e i).
div.p: For obtaining the stability result remark that using ( 7), ( 12)-( 14) and 0 ) ( " ΎΑ╛ r z for the coefficients of the Jacobi equation the following inequalities can be obtained: ii). For obtaining the instability result remark now that for the coefficients of the Jacobi equation the following inequalities hold: .This means that the Jacobi condition for weak minimum is not satisfied [27].
div.formula: n R z r g d / ) sin ( ] ) ' ( 1 [ 3 2 / 3 2 ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒπ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΓ│ ΎΑτ ΎΔΩ d R g r g ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΓμ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒ▓ Therefore the equation 0 ' sin 3 ΎΑ╜ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΑτ ΎΔ╖ ΎΔ╕ ΎΔ╢ ΎΔπ ΎΔρ ΎΔο ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΔΩ ΎΒΨ ΎΒ▓ ΎΒΨ ΎΒκ ΎΒπ d g d R g n
c d R z r ΎΒκ ΎΒπ ΎΒπ

div.head: ACKNOWLEDGEMENT
div.p: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Sturm type upper bound" ([28] Chapter 11) for the Jacobi equation . An arbitrary solution of the above "Sturm type upper bound equation" is given by ) that the Jacobi condition for weak minimum is satisfied [27].

div.p: Hence, by the mean value theorem, there exists
, 'ΎΔΟ such that the following equality holds:
is strictly increasing and by consequence the function ) (r ΎΒκ is strictly decreasing. Therefore,

monogr.title: Growth of fiber Crystals" Crystal Growth of Electronic Materials
